SUMMARY:
Recycling technologies, policies, prospects, and challenges for spent batteries - ScienceDirect Besides, the waste battery recycling industry, through processes involving sorting, extraction, and reuse of valuable metals, not only generates employment opportunities and drives economic development, but also reduces the manufacturing costs of new batteries and enhances the overall sustainability of the battery industry.27 Consequently, waste batteries harbor valuable resources such as Li, Ni, Co, Fe, and Mn (see Figure 2C), which can be effectively recycled and reused, thereby curbing the excessive exploitation and consumption of natural resources.24 Therefore, mere disposal of spent batteries is deemed undesirable, as it fails to capitalize on the valuable elements contained within them and causes irreversible harm to the ecosystem and human population.

Review

Recycling technologies, policies, prospects, and challenges for spent batteries
=======================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Zhuang Kang 1, Zhixin Huang 2, Qingguo Peng 1 2, Zhiwei Shi 2, Huaqiang Xiao 1, Ruixue Yin 1, Guang Fu 1, Jin Zhao 1

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Summary
-------

The recycling of spent [batteries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an important concern in resource conservation and environmental protection, while it is facing challenges such as insufficient recycling channels, high costs, and technical difficulties. To address these issues, a review of the recycling of spent batteries, emphasizing the importance and potential value of recycling is conducted. Besides, the recycling policies and strategies implemented in representative countries are summarized, providing legal and policy support for the recycling industry. Moreover, a comprehensive classification and comparison of recycling technologies identify the characteristics and current status of different approaches. The integrated recycling technology provides a better recycling performance with zero-pollution recycling of spent battery. Biorecycling technology is expected to gain a broad development prospect in the future owing to the superiority of energy-saving and environmental protection, high recycling efficiency, via [microbial degradation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/microbial-biodegradation "Learn more about microbial degradation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [enzymatic degradation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/enzymatic-degradation "Learn more about enzymatic degradation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), etc. Consequently, as for the existing recycling challenges of waste batteries, developing new recycling technology and perfecting its recycling system is an indispensable guarantee for the [sustainable development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainable development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of waste battery. Meanwhile, theoretical support is offered for the recycling of spent batteries.

-------------

Electrochemical energy storage

Environmental policy

Engineering

Introduction
------------

Energy saving and emission control is a hot topic because of the shortage of [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-resource "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the continuous augmentation of greenhouse gases.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib1) So, sustainable energy sources, solar energy,[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib2)[tidal energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/tidal-power "Learn more about tidal energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib3) biomass,[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib4) power [battery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about battery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib5) and other emerging energy sources are available and a zero-carbon target is proposed.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib6) Actually, the major contributor of [greenhouse gas emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/greenhouse-gas-emission "Learn more about greenhouse gas emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), i.e., [fuel combustion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/fuel-combustion "Learn more about fuel combustion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or engines, is gradually replaced by battery-driven.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib7) Currently, there exist many types of power batteries, i.e., lead-acid, Ni-Cd, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), and LiFePO 4 batteries,[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib8) etc. Also, the development of power battery technology is maturing, which leads to the prevalence of electric vehicles (EVs) in the near future.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib9) Meanwhile, it causes an increased demand for raw materials for power cells. Therefore, spent batteries and valuable metal substances should be fully recycled to prevent a shortage of raw materials,[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib10) as presented in [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig1).

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (578KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (578KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 1. Developments of the battery

(A) Life cycle chain of LIBs. Copyright 2023, Elsevier, Reproduced with permission.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib9)

(B) The [circular economy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/circular-economy "Learn more about circular economy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of waste LIBs. Copyright 2023, Elsevier, Reproduced with permission.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib11)

Spent batteries primarily consist of abundant substances, i.e., Al, Cu, Fe, Mn, Co, Ni, etc., which not only result in [environmental pollution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-pollution "Learn more about environmental pollution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") but also pose risks to human life and health.[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib12) Therefore, the recycling of spent batteries holds significant importance, and extensive research has been conducted on the recycling of spent batteries. Kang et al.[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib13) conducted comprehensive studies and employed standardized [leaching tests](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/leaching-test "Learn more about leaching tests from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [life cycle impact assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/life-cycle-impact-assessment "Learn more about life cycle impact assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and hazard assessment models to evaluate the environmental hazards caused by used [lithium batteries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/lithium-battery "Learn more about lithium batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The research indicates that Co, Cu, Ni, and Ti are responsible for causing environmental impacts. Besides, Yan et al.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib14) proposed a two-step crushing method, and demonstrated its superior crushing efficiency and environmental friendliness compared to single tear crushing and single hammer crushing techniques. In a similar vein, Shin et al.[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib15) developed a [froth flotation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flotation-froth "Learn more about froth flotation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology for active material separation, showcasing its ability to retain a significant portion of electrochemical activity in the recovered positive active material. This technology can be readily implemented in a direct recovery process. Li et al.[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib16) introduced a novel [pyrometallurgical process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pyrometallurgical-process "Learn more about pyrometallurgical process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") known as low-temperature alkali pool smelting, which effectively recovers Pb from spent lactic acid bacteria. Liu et al.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib17) adopted the interval plant-wide hierarchical optimization methodology for uncertainty optimization of hydrometallurgical plant-wide processes to reduce economic losses. Liivand et al.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib18) proposed a pioneering approach to drive the green transition by utilizing graphite recovered from spent batteries. Furthermore, Kastanaki et al.[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib19) suggested a [circular economy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/circular-economy "Learn more about circular economy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") business model to regulate the [remanufacture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/remanufacture "Learn more about remanufacture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), reuse, and recycling of [spent LIBs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/spent-lithium-ion-battery "Learn more about spent LIBs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Gu et al.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib5) introduced a government incentive and punishment model to impact battery market recycling rates. Various recycling technologies are depicted, i.e., physical recycling, direct recycling, pyrometallurgical, and [hydrometallurgy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/hydrometallurgy "Learn more about hydrometallurgy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") recycling methods, which promote the green transformation. Hence, the waste [battery recycling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/battery-recycling "Learn more about battery recycling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") industry holds significant potential for application and development.

The recycling of waste batteries faces several challenges, including the establishment of effective recycling channels, high recycling costs, and technical complexities. To tackle these obstacles and present an efficient and green recycling process for spent batteries, a review of recycling technologies, policies, prospects and challenges is conducted. In this work, the significance of battery recycling, policies, and strategies are emphasized in Section [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#sec2), and techniques, challenges of recycling are considered in Section [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#sec3) and Section [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#sec4), respectively. Besides, the recycling policies and strategies of the EU, [USA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/united-states-of-america "Learn more about USA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and China are summarized to provide legal support and policy protection for the recycling industry. By comprehensively reviewing the recycling technologies and conducting a comparative analysis of approaches, valuable insights into the current status and distinguishing features of various recycling methods can be obtained. It provides some perspectives for the development and innovation of recycling technologies.

Spent battery recycling and strategies
--------------------------------------

### Effects of battery recycling

[Batteries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about Batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") achieve extensive applications across diverse fields, including electricity, transportation, and daily living, as depicted in [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig2)A.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib20) The widespread adoption of batteries is driven by the need to address the [detrimental effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/detrimental-effect "Learn more about detrimental effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of oil-based transportation and associated exhaust emissions, which are significant contributors to [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the heightened strain on [natural resource](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-resource "Learn more about natural resource from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") availability.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib7) Nevertheless, the average service life of batteries is typically limited to 4–6 years, leading to a significant accumulation of waste stockpiles primarily due to inadequate disposal practices, as illustrated in [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig2)B.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib21) It is worth noting that China is projected to generate over 45,000 tons of discarded batteries by 2025, with a total weight exceeding 50,000 tons.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib22) This statistic underscores the immense potential and prospects within the waste battery industry. However, due to the increasing demand for batteries, the market necessitated over 100 kilotons of Li materials in 2020.[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib23) So, the reuse of spent batteries is one significant approach to address the large demand of raw materials for manufacturing, as indicated in [Figures 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig2)C and 2D.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib24) Hence, it becomes imperative to put forth an efficient and environmentally friendly approach to [battery recycling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/battery-recycling "Learn more about battery recycling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

![Image 13](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (649KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (649KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004223021491-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 2. Current and future development of battery

(A) Application scenarios. Copyright 2021, Elsevier, Reproduced with permission.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib20)

(B) Global production trends of electric vehicles. Copyright 2012, IEEE, Reproduced with permission.[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib25)

(C) Valuable metals in spent batteries. Copyright 2021, Elsevier, Reproduced with permission.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib24)

(D) Waste batteries in China. Copyright 2017, Springer Nature, Reproduced with permission.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib21)

An effective closed-loop recycling chain is illustrated in [Figures 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig1)A and 1B, where valuable materials are recycled in battery gradient utilization.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib9) The improper handling of batteries, in turn, has adverse impacts on both human beings and the environment. Notably, the toxic chemical substances of batteries lead to pollution of soil, water, and air, consequently jeopardizing human health and the ecological equilibrium.[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib26) Hence, recycling of waste battery is an urgent matter. Besides, the waste battery recycling industry, through processes involving sorting, extraction, and reuse of valuable metals, not only generates employment opportunities and drives economic development, but also reduces the manufacturing costs of new batteries and enhances the overall [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the battery industry.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib27) Consequently, waste batteries [harbor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ports-and-harbors "Learn more about harbor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") valuable resources such as Li, Ni, Co, Fe, and Mn (see [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#fig2)C), which can be effectively recycled and reused, thereby curbing the excessive exploitation and consumption of natural resources.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib24) Therefore, mere disposal of spent batteries is deemed undesirable, as it fails to capitalize on the valuable elements contained within them and causes irreversible harm to the ecosystem and human population. By implementing efficient and environmentally friendly methods for battery recycling, it becomes possible to maximize the recovery of valuable materials, reduce [environmental pollution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-pollution "Learn more about environmental pollution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), stimulate economic growth, and conserve precious natural resources. Moreover, it is advantageous for the sustainable development of the battery industry.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib21)

### Policies and strategies

With the global proliferation of EVs, the establishment of policies for the recycling of spent batteries has become crucial. Simultaneously, the standardization and management of the waste battery industry have been duly influenced, prompting countries to enact regulations to govern the management and processing procedures of spent batteries.[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib28) So, an overview of pertinent policies and regulations of the EU, the US, and China as illustrative examples is presented, and the green recycling routes with high efficiency is pursued.

#### European Union

EU first pays attention to recycling, which has formulated special laws and regulations on waste batteries.[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib29) In 1991, Council Directive 91/157/EEC was issued on cells policy of waste cells and storage of batteries with Hg, Cd, and Pb.[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib30) The Netherlands stipulated that manufacturers and importers of [mobile phone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/mobile-phone "Learn more about mobile phone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") batteries must be solely responsible for collecting and producing in 1995. Then, battery retailers are obligated to recycle used cells in Denmark, Sweden, and other European countries, and they implemented a special excise tax of 6–8% on batteries sold. According to ref. [31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib31), the recycling rate of waste batteries and mobile phone batteries has exceeded 75% in Denmark and 95% in Sweden. [Germany](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/germany "Learn more about Germany from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") actively implements the Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) directives 2006/66/EC and 2012/19/EU, forming a legal system for waste [LIBs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/lithium-ion-battery "Learn more about LIBs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") management.[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib32) In 2015, the Foundation for Common Recycling System recycled 45.9% of the spent portable cells in Germany and the recycling rate of the waste [LIBs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/lithium-ion-batteries "Learn more about LIBs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reached 100%.[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib33) Meanwhile, in recent years, EU also has been pursuing a goal of net [greenhouse gas emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/greenhouse-gas-emission "Learn more about greenhouse gas emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reductions of more than 55% by 2030, and additionally aims to collect 45% of portable batteries by 2023, 63% by 2027, and 73% by 2030, and 51% of LMT (Light Transportation Vehicle Batteries) by 2028, and 61% by 2031.[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib34)

#### USA

[USA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/united-states-of-america "Learn more about USA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") issued the Resource Conservation and Restoration Act (RCRA) in 1976, and established a framework for [hazardous waste management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/hazardous-waste-management "Learn more about hazardous waste management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib35) Particularly, New York and California are the forerunners of the US in LIBs recycling.[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib36) In 2006, California Battery Recycling Act (AB1125) was enacted, requiring the establishment of a battery collection system for multi-purpose rechargeable batteries to recover and legally reuse retired rechargeable batteries.[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib37) An unprofitable social service agency, USA “Rechargeable Battery Recycling Corporation”, is established and responsible for operating spent battery recycling.[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib38) Moreover, the USA also introduces the awareness of WEEE management to education, which is conducive to the development of waste battery management.[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004223021491#bib36) In particular, California is planning to end the sale of gas/diesel-fueled cars by 2035. Besides, owing to the rapidly increased EVs, the “Battery Safety Initiative for Electric Vehicles” in 2023 mandates that data related to [EV batteries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electric-vehicle "Learn more about EV batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is collected to understand safety-related battery defects and improve the safety of EVs.[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258900422302149

## The Environmental Impact of Battery Disposal

![](https://internationalfireandsafetyjournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/battery-solar-energy-storage-system.jpg)
![Picture of Iain Hoey](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20300%20300'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Picture of Iain Hoey](https://internationalfireandsafetyjournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Iain-Hoey-Headshot-300x300.jpg)

#### Iain Hoey

#### Share this content

## Understanding the Environmental Consequences of Battery Disposal

Batteries are ubiquitous in our modern world, but their disposal presents significant environmental challenges.

As the demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) increases, driven by the rise in electric transportation and renewable energy storage, the volume of battery waste also grows.

The lack of universal standards for waste disposal of LIBs across the globe results in varying practices, including landfilling, incineration, and full or partial recycling.

However, these methods are not without [drawbacks](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2021/ee/d1ee00691f)​​.

## The Hidden Environmental Cost of Batteries

The [environmental](https://renewablesadvice.com/battery/environmental-impact/) impact of batteries extends beyond disposal.

The production process of lithium-ion batteries is energy-intensive and contributes significantly to carbon emissions.

The extraction of lithium, a key component of these batteries, is a water-intensive process that has led to protests and mining halts due to environmental concerns.

Manufacturing processes for batteries also require large amounts of energy and involve hazardous chemicals.

The recycling of lithium batteries, while a growing trend, remains inefficient and resource-intensive​​.

## The Wider Impact of Battery Production and Disposal

### The Global Lithium Market and Environmental Effects

The lithium-ion battery [market](https://www.trvst.world/environment/environmental-impact-of-battery-production-and-disposal/), valued at US$30 billion in 2017, is projected to reach $100 billion by 2025.

Battery production requires the extraction of materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel, leading to significant environmental and social impacts.

For instance, lithium extraction in regions like South America and East Asia uses large quantities of water and toxic chemicals, leading to environmental degradation and community displacement.

Cobalt mining, primarily in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, involves destructive practices and raises serious human rights concerns​​.

### Battery Waste and Environmental Challenges

Improperly disposed batteries contribute to environmental pollution.

As they corrode, their chemicals leach into the soil and water, contaminating ecosystems.

Lithium batteries, in particular, can be volatile and cause landfill fires, releasing harmful gases into the atmosphere.

The disposal of electric vehicle batteries poses additional challenges due to their size and complexity.

If not dismantled correctly, the hazardous materials within these batteries can explode​​.

### IFSJ Comment

The issue of [battery](https://internationalfireandsafetyjournal.com/battery-energy-storage-system-hazards/) disposal and its environmental impact is multifaceted and global in scale.

The increasing reliance on batteries, particularly lithium-ion, for various applications from electric vehicles to renewable energy storage, underscores the urgency of addressing these challenges.

Effective management of battery waste requires a comprehensive approach, including the development of more efficient recycling technologies, the implementation of global standards for battery disposal, and the advancement of less environmentally harmful battery technologies.

Furthermore, mitigating the environmental impact of battery production, especially in the extraction of materials like lithium and cobalt, is critical.

It involves not only technological solutions but also ethical considerations, particularly in terms of human rights and ecological preservation.

# The Impact of Battery Recycling on the Environment

Most of our everyday electronics make use of batteries, and they can easily be found in bulk at any general store. Because of this, many businesses hardly give a second thought about simply tossing old, useless batteries into the garbage. What they don’t realize, however, is the harm they can cause to the environment. Learn about the impact of battery recycling on the environment and why it’s so important.

When you throw away an old battery, you might think nothing of it except for maybe a quick “oh well” or “someone else will take care of this one” before walking away from it. However, there are many hidden costs associated with throwing away a battery that you may not even be aware of until after it has been thrown in the trash.

#### Keeps Batteries Out of Landfills

First and foremost, when you recycle your batteries, you’re keeping them out of landfills. Landfills are one of the most harmful ways to dispose of waste because they can cause serious environmental damage. Batteries that end up in landfills may eventually corrode and decay, releasing dangerous chemicals into the environment—including battery acid! These chemicals can seep into our soil, water sources, and even the air. This is bad for both our environment and our health.

But what if someone else has to get these chemicals out of their property? That’s where recycling comes in. When we recycle old batteries properly, we’re not only keeping them from sitting in landfills where they may eventually corrode and decay; we’re also getting rid of any harmful chemicals that might be inside those batteries!

#### Consumes Fewer Resources

Some core battery materials include copper and aluminum. These are materials that we can recycle and reuse endlessly, ensuring that we can continue to create batteries while consuming far fewer resources. The impact of battery recycling on the environment here is crucial. We can avoid depleting the environment of natural resources and prevent shortages from occurring—ensuring batteries remain plentiful and affordable.

Aluminum has a long history as an important resource in manufacturing, but it is only recently that we have begun to realize its true potential as a battery material. It has been used in batteries since 1888, but only recently have we begun to conduct research on its viability as a replacement for lead-acid batteries. Copper is another important metal used in creating batteries today. It has been used in batteries since 1887, but unlike aluminum, copper hasn’t been extensively studied yet. We’re hoping to change that with our new technology!

#### Recycling Made Easy

Recycling batteries is a simple way to improve the planet, but it can be difficult to know where to turn when you’re done using them. Fortunately, there’s Cirba Solutions. We’re experts in recycling and disposing of old batteries, and we’ve got everything you need to make sure that your  batteries are recycled safely.

The best part? We handle every aspect of the recycling process, so your business doesn’t have to worry about it at all. That means less work for you and more time doing what you love! Contact us today to learn more about our services or keep reading for more information about how we can help your business recycle batteries in a way that maximizes efficiency and minimizes cost.

# The truth about EV battery recycling

![electric vehicle battery recycling charging station charger chargepoint charge.png](https://images.here.com/x7rx8ayph7ee/8c8ba018-6d04-4975-b05a-88e4da21ade0/8f1f4d0ad6e68e34ce8533fe90299058/electric-vehicle-battery-recycling-charging-station-charger-chargepoint-charge.png?w=827&q=80&fm=webp)

#### Despite electric vehicles promising us a cleaner future, the industry's struggle to recycle batteries is proving to be a significant challenge.

[Electric vehicles (EVs)](https://here.com/solutions/connected-driving/electric-vehicle) are more popular than ever. The EU forecasts that by 2020, over 30 million of them will be on European roads. And as that number increases, so will the volume of batteries reaching the end of their life span.

But their batteries pose a unique problem. Unlike a traditional car engine that can be rebuilt, an EV battery will inevitably wear out. Depending on specific usage and brand, they need to be replaced every ten years.

![woman driver electric vehicle ev charging station charge point](https://images.here.com/x7rx8ayph7ee/d5a89313-e0e4-4f07-a7f9-72e498e5d724/253bc5c85110793ddb8514dc836d7042/woman-driver-electric-vehicle-ev-charging-station-charge-point.png?w=1024&q=80&fm=webp)

Read more — [EVs in range: how far can an electric car go on a single charge?](/learn/blog/ev-one-charge)

## Why recycling is the key to sustainability

Manufacturing EV batteries is an energy-intensive process that produces [greenhouse gas emissions](/learn/blog/ev-battery-sustainability-recycling). And if they're not disposed of carefully, this could negate any positive environmental impact of driving an electric vehicle.

EV batteries also contain toxic materials such as [lithium](/learn/blog/lithium-ion-vs-lithium-iron-phosphate), cobalt and nickel. If left in landfills they can leak into the soil and groundwater, posing serious environmental hazards. But if recycled, these toxic materials can be reused to manufacture new batteries, which also saves us from having to mine for new ones.

## EV batteries are not that easy to recycle

Despite best efforts, in the last decade the rates of recycling EV batteries have been low. That's because – although there is a clear need – the current state of EV battery recycling is far from ideal. It involves several challenges, including their complex design, varying chemistries and the lack of standardized recycling processes.

The main barrier is that the current recycling process is complicated and costly. Geographical differences in recycling infrastructure complicate logistics can make the whole process inefficient and expensive. Disassembling batteries requires specialized facilities and skilled labor, raising costs. And the fluctuating prices of recycled materials often make mining new ones more financially appealing.

Without strong economic incentives or regulations to support recycling, the industry finds it hard to improve sustainable battery disposal and reuse.

![lithium air battery road distance ev range anxiety](https://images.here.com/x7rx8ayph7ee/fbc15cad-4481-4f34-b5f0-f41bdb438077/d15ee71ba82d25fd303805ef93feed86/lithium-air-battery-road-distance-ev-range-anxiety.jpg?w=1024&q=80&fm=webp)

Read more — [EV does it: will lithium-air batteries solve range anxiety?](/learn/blog/lithium-air-batteries-ev-range-anxiety)

## How businesses are tackling the issue

Several companies and organizations are working hard to address these challenges and improve the recycling rates of EV batteries. For instance, Nissan launched its first EV, the LEAF, in 2010 and began preparing for the battery's end-of-life cycle shortly after. [Nissan partnered with Sumitomo Corporation](/learn/blog/nissan-4r-energy) to establish 4R Energy Corp., specializing in repurposing lithium-ion batteries.

4R Energy uses innovative technology to examine a pack of 48 EV modules at once, reducing assessment time from two weeks to one day. This partnership aims to ensure each EV battery reaches its full potential through it's motto of "[Reuse, Resell, Refabricate and Recycle](/learn/blog/ev-battery-recycling)". Additionally, Volkswagen has a recycling plant in Salzgitter, Germany, planning to recycle up to 3,600 battery systems per year.

Another promising approach to making EV batteries more sustainable is to give them a second life. Once an EV battery is no longer suitable for use in vehicles, it can still be repurposed for other applications. For example, [Betteries, a company in Berlin,](/learn/blog/lithium-ion-battery-recycling) uses EV batteries to create new energy sources, producing up to ten second-life 'betteries' from one regular EV battery. These [repurposed batteries](/learn/blog/seven-ways-your-ev-battery-can-be-reused-before-its-recycled) can be used in smaller units like forklifts or clean energy storage systems.

The next step in improving EV battery recycling involves investment and collaboration between governments and industry stakeholders. Curtiss, [a researcher at the Argonne National Laboratory](/learn/blog/lithium-air-batteries-ev-range-anxiety), suggests that with adequate funding, the development of new technologies like lithium-air batteries could revolutionize the EV industry. These batteries promise a longer range and lighter weight, which could drastically change how we approach EV battery recycling in the future.

![man plugging in ev charger charging station](https://images.here.com/x7rx8ayph7ee/64b2fcc0-8fe0-48af-836a-c452b16b726b/9d39123c685753f42d75401193528a9a/man-plugging-in-ev-charger-charging-station.jpg?w=1024&q=80&fm=webp)

Read more — [EVs in range: how fast is adoption growing globally?](/learn/blog/electric-vehicle-expansion)

## Moving toward a more sustainable future

The journey to achieving effective EV battery recycling is fraught with challenges, but it is essential for the [sustainability of the electric vehicle](/learn/blog/ev-batteries-sustainability) industry and the environment. Through innovative technologies, second-life applications, and collaborative efforts, significant strides can be made to improve recycling rates and reduce the environmental impact of EV batteries.

Understanding and addressing the complexities of EV battery recycling is crucial for moving toward a greener future. By investing in research and development, supporting innovative recycling methods and fostering industry collaboration, we can ensure that the green promise of electric vehicles is fully realized.

![Portrait of Caroline Christie](https://images.here.com/x7rx8ayph7ee/1d7155d5-02c5-45e0-b37b-7145a6dd8745/b11981cf1906479946fca41f4ee4e458/caroline_christie.jpeg?w=140&q=80&fm=webp)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Improper disposal of batteries leads to environmental hazards, as toxic substances can leak into soil and water, causing contamination and health risks.
Batteries contribute to pollution when not recycled, releasing harmful chemicals into ecosystems, affecting wildlife, water quality, and human health.
Emerging recycling technologies, such as biorecycling and low-temperature processes, improve efficiency and reduce pollution, allowing for better recovery of valuable materials from spent batteries.
Regulatory frameworks are crucial for establishing recycling standards; policies promote responsible battery disposal and incentivize recycling initiatives to mitigate environmental impacts.
A circular economy approach encourages the reuse and recycling of battery materials, reducing the demand for virgin resources and minimizing waste, fostering sustainability.
